# MCP Tool Design: Why Your AI Agent Is Failing (And How to Fix It)

## MCP Best Practices (5 Part Series)

## The Reports of MCP's Death Have Been Greatly Exaggerated

Scroll through developer forums in early 2026, and you'll find a recurring theme: MCP is dead. The takes range from dismissive ("just a fad") to resigned ("we tried it, our agents kept failing"). And the frustrations behind them are real. Teams are building MCP servers with 50+ tools, watching their agents stumble through tool selection, and concluding that the protocol itself is broken.

It isn't. MCP isn't dead; it's being used poorly. And the evidence for how to use it well is now overwhelming. [...] This iterative loop is where MCP shines compared to direct API integration. Changing a tool's name, description, or input schema is a server-side change, with no client updates, no SDK version bumps, no breaking changes propagated to consumers. The MCP protocol decouples tool discovery from tool invocation, so the LLM rediscovers the improved schema on the next connection. This makes the feedback cycle fast: observe failures, update the tool design, deploy, and measure again. Teams that treat tool design as a one-time exercise miss the biggest advantage of having MCP in the middle. You should put effort into designing the MCP server correctly, as "You never get a second chance to make a first impression." However, you should continue to monitor the MCP server usage logs to adjust to the [...] The parallels between UX design and MCP tool design run deep. Decades of UX research have produced principles that transfer directly:

Affordance - the idea that a door handle should look pullable, maps to tool names and parameter descriptions: if a field is named `id` but requires a UUID, the affordance is broken.

`id`

Recognition over recall - it's easier to pick from a list than type from memory, maps to using enums and example values in schemas so the LLM recognizes valid inputs instead of guessing.

Visibility of system status - users need feedback when something goes wrong, maps to error messages that explain what happened and how to fix it, rather than a cryptic "invalid input." These aren't metaphors. They're the same design discipline applied to a different kind of user.

So, we're going to look at this post from Luca, who is the author of the blog post we're going to discuss later in this video. So, he says, "MCP is all fun until you add this one malicious MCP server and forget about it. We have discovered a critical flaw in the widely used model context protocol or MCP that enables a new form of LLM attack we term tool poisoning. This could leak SSH keys, API keys, and etc. So, how exactly this attack happens? When you add a new MCP server, the host will get a list of all the tools as well as their description, and those are going to be added to the context of the LLM. results.title: MCP Apps in ChatGPT are fundamentally broken - 2 critical bugs - ChatGPT Apps SDK - OpenAI Developer Community
MCP Is Broken and Anthropic Just Admitted It
results.content:
You can bundle a lot of knowledge into a skill. Multiple markdown files. Examples. Metric definitions. Even full Python scripts that act like deterministic microservices inside the agent’s environment. But none of that hits the context window until Claude decides it’s relevant for the current task.

That is the opposite of how most MCP setups behave.

And Skills aren’t just a Claude UI trick. They’re supported across Claude apps, Claude Code, the API, and the Agent SDK, all wired into the same code execution tool that gives agents a secure environment to run scripts.

So instead of saying “MCP is broken,” Anthropic did something much more interesting: they changed how the model _meets_ MCP.

Skills sit in front. MCP sits behind.

## Skills = Retrieval. MCP = Tools. Together = RAG-MCP. [...] That’s RAG-MCP in practice:

1.   Retrieve the right skill.
2.   Load only the instructions and references that matter.
3.   Execute code for deterministic steps.
4.   Call MCP tools as a last-mile integration when the workflow requires it.

Skills don’t “compete” with MCP.

They domesticate it.

## Why This Matters for People Actually Building Agents

If you’re just wiring up toy projects, you can brute-force a lot of this. Context windows are large, demos are short, tools are few. MCP feels fine.

As soon as you step into:

   multi-tenant systems,
   long-lived conversations,
   high-stakes workflows (compliance, finance, safety),
   or multi-agent networks where tools call tools,

the cracks show.

You start to see: [...] They quietly shipped a mechanism that accepts the reality of large tool ecosystems and gives agents a way to interact with them without collapsing under the weight.

Skills are composable.

Portable across products.

Efficient because they only load what they need.

And powerful because they can bring code execution into the mix whenever reliability matters more than pure generation.

Most importantly, they acknowledge the thing many of us have felt building in the wild:

The future of agents is not a bigger pile of tools.

It is a better way of _relating_ to tools.

One where retrieval and orchestration come first.

Where context is treated as a scarce resource, not an infinite dumping ground.

Where MCP is still there, but it’s no longer sitting on the model’s chest.

results.title: MCP Servers Explained: Why Most Are Useless (And How to Fix It)
results.content: be able to do. They're not, but imagine that they are. It should not matter that pushing with the CLI failed for reasons somehow related to the repo being created through the MCB. We'll ignore that as well. I want us to avoid complaining about the quality of those MCPS. As you will see soon, such complaints would be a waste of time. Instead, let's do one more operation with those MCPS by instructing the agent to delete the GitHub repo we just created. And there we go. GitHub MCP MCP, remember, as expected, did exactly what we asked it to do. How about that? Wasn't that awesome, right? Amazing. We extended capabilities of the agent by letting it use Git and GitHub MCP and as a result the agent managed to do almost almost everything we needed it to do related to Git. Great, right? But wait, [...] to create an app and it knows that it can use that specific MCP for that specific intent. The agent has a specific task to perform that would be difficult to do using existing CLIs and existing knowledge in the model. That's a scenario where MCP helps. Okay. So now that we have a clearer idea what are and what are not good use cases for MCPS, let's discuss what should be inside those servers. And here's where most people get it completely wrong. Most people think of MCP servers as code that agents use to do something, right? The agent executes instructions from a model and MCP provides a unified interface that the agent can use to do something or get some data. MCP is essentially a server like any other. It's data in, data out, like most other types of applications. Think of it like a [...] to operations and resource access and MCP should have. For example, we might want to restrict it to work only within a specific directory or to communicate only with specific APIs. When we need something like that, we can use roots which are loaded when MCP connection is established. Roots serve as a security an organization mechanism. It is telling the MCP server exactly what it can and cannot access. Think of them as a white list of allowed resources. Here's an example. In this example, the MCP server can only access files within the backend service directory and the specific GitHub repository. Any attempt to access files outside those boundaries should be rejected by the MCP server. Maybe this is probably the weakest story of MCP altogether. Slight problem or maybe not slight a big
